contentBody: For a single search query on an unsorted array of N elements, which approach is more efficient?
option.1: Sort the array first, then binary search
option.4: It depends on the target value
answerExplanation: Sorting adds O(N log N) overhead; for one query, linear O(N) is better.

contentBody: For N = 1,000,000 elements and Q queries, at approximately what value of Q does sorting once and using binary search become faster?
answerExplanation: Break-even when N log N ≈ QN → Q ≈ log N ≈ 20.

contentBody: In binary search with lo = 2,000,000,000 and hi = 2,000,000,001, which formula correctly computes mid without overflow?
answerExplanation: This avoids overflow in languages with fixed integer size.

contentBody: What is the maximum number of iterations binary search requires on an array of 128 elements?
Which statement best explains why binary search runs in O(log N)?
mcscAnswer: It divides the search space by 2 each iteration, reducing a size-N problem to size 1 in log₂(N) steps
option.2: It divides the search space by 2 each iteration, reducing a size-N problem to size 1 in log₂(N) steps
option.3: It uses hashing to find elements quickly
option.4: It sorts the array before searching
answerExplanation: Each step halves the search space.

contentBody: Binary search on arr = [3,6,9,12,15,18,21], target = 18 (lo=0, hi=6, mid=3, arr[3]=12). What is the next state?
Binary search on arr = [1,2,4,8], target = 3 (not present). After all iterations, what condition ends the loop?
answerExplanation: Loop ends when search space becomes invalid (lo > hi).
